retrieved_docs.title: CT-scan Image Production Procedures -- Issues of Concern -- Summary of Hardware Functions
retrieved_docs.content: The generator produces an electrical current that runs into the X-ray tube and through the cathode wire filament. The current passes its electrons to the filament. The difference in electric charge between the cathode filament and anode across the vacuum draws the electrons toward the anode. Two distinct reactions in the anode convert 1% of the electrons that strike it into X-ray photons. These photons are directed through the scanned patient. The shielding elements help to reduce the scattered radiations and reduce the artifacts in the produced images. The movement of the table through the gantry allows a complete scanning of the desired part of the patient. The degree of X-ray beam absorption (also called attenuation) differs according to the density of each anatomical structure through which the beam passes. The detector receives X-ray photons with different energy intensities and converts them to visual light photons and then to an electrical signal. The electrical signal is conditioned by an electron amplifier and converted from a continuous (analog) signal into discrete (digital) signals by an analog-to-digital converter.

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Image Production Equipment Operation -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Factors affecting X-ray emission include the anode material, the X-ray tube voltage, current, beam filtration, and generator waveform. The anode target material affects the efficiency of X-ray radiation production. The output is roughly proportional to the atomic number of the anode material. Besides, the X-rays' energies also depend on the target material. The tube voltage (kV) dictates the maximum energy of the produced photons. An increase in the voltage results in increased X-ray production efficiency, quantity, and quality of the resulting X-ray beam. X-ray beam intensity (or the number of photons in the beam) is directly proportional to the tube current and exposure time. [7] The only parameter that can change the shape of the X-ray spectrum is voltage. [2] [8] Beam filtration changes the quantity and quality of the X-ray beam by preferentially filtering low-energy photons. The current required to achieve the desired X-ray intensity is higher for more filtered beams. Finally, the generator waveform affects the quality of the produced X-ray spectrum. The output X-ray spectrum is of higher quality and quantity for the same voltage, current, and exposure time.

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Image Production Equipment Operation -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The cathode is the source of electrons. It is the negative electrode in an X-ray tube and, most commonly, made of a tungsten filament. The size of the filament used depends on the factors of the technique.  When energy is applied, the filament heats up; electrons build at the cathode, and, through a process called thermionic emission, these electrons are released from the filament surface. The rate of electron release is determined by the current applied and the filament's temperature. Electrons accelerate toward the positively charged anode. [2] The anode is where electrons decelerate, and the energy from deceleration is released through heat and X-rays (photons).  The X-ray tube's output is emission-limited, and the filament current dictates the X-ray tube current. The tube current is proportional to the x-ray flux at any tube voltage applied. The emitted electrons are focused into a concentrated group accelerated toward the anode, striking a small area called the focal spot. The filament length and electron distribution determine the focal spot's size.

retrieved_docs.title: CT-scan Image Production Procedures -- Issues of Concern -- X-ray Tube
retrieved_docs.content: The X-ray tube converts moving electrons (i.e., electricity) into photons with the energetic properties (the wavelength and amplitude) of X-rays. The X-ray tube is composed of a cathode assembly, an anode assembly, and a rotor, all contained in a tube envelope and together forming a structure called the tube insert. All gas atoms in the space inside the tube envelope have been evacuated, forming a vacuum. [4] Modern CT scanner X-ray tubes are usually provided with 20-60 kilowatts of electrical power.

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Image Production Equipment Operation -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The final piece of equipment used to produce X-rays is the tube generator. The X-ray generator provides an electrical current at a high voltage to the X-ray tube, producing an X-ray beam. [1] A fundamental principle of electromagnetic induction is that a constantly applied current through a wire or coil produces a constant magnetic field. Variations in the current produce variations in the magnetic field. Transformers use electromagnetic induction to change the voltage of an electrical power source. They increase (step-up), decrease (step-down), or leave unchanged (isolate) the input voltage depending on the voltage required by the X-ray generator.

retrieved_docs.title: CT-scan Image Production Procedures -- Issues of Concern -- Generator
retrieved_docs.content: The generator provides the electrical power that is necessary to generate X-rays using two types of electrical current. A high voltage (20-150 kilovolts) supply determines the maximum intensity of the X-rays that can be produced. Increasing this voltage increases the electrical potential difference between the anode and cathode. A low fixed voltage (about 10 kilovolts) supply to the cathode filament enables continuous electron emission through a thermionic reaction, which is discussed below. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Production Technical Evaluation -- Issues of Concern -- mAs
retrieved_docs.content: Increasing the mAs of an X-ray comes from increasing the current being sent through the X-ray tube's cathode, which results in increased photons in the X-ray beam being shot toward the patient. Too low mAs may result in "quantum mottle," which is essentially X-ray noise. In this scenario, there are not enough photons reaching the X-ray cassette, and important details from images may be missing or unable to be seen. On the other hand, when mAs are too high, more photons are being shot from the X-ray tube, and the patient gets overexposure to radiation. An additional factor to consider when manipulating mAs is that it is a time-dependent variable. If the electrical current is held constant, the amount of photons a patient is exposed to entirely depends on the time they are in front of an X-ray beam. Ideally, a patient is imaged in as little time as possible with a higher electrical current to reduce motion artifact during an X-ray.

retrieved_docs.title: X-Ray Image Quality Assurance -- Issues of Concern -- Tube Current-Exposure Time Product
retrieved_docs.content: Tube current determines the total photons impinging the patient to form an image. Milliampere seconds (mAs) is the product of tube current in milliamperes and exposure time in seconds. There is a linear relationship between mAs and patient dose. An increase in mAs leads to increased patient dose and a noise reduction. Depending on the clinical need, appropriate mAs should be selected for a given exam to optimize the balance between noise and dose. Exposure time can affect spatial resolution as long exposure times can increase the chances of patient motion, leading to image blur. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Image Production Equipment Operation -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The X-ray tube is the source of the X-ray beam. Its basic components include a cathode, anode, rotor, envelope, tube port, cable sockets, and tube housing. The generator allows the radiology technologist to control 3 technique factors: the tube voltage applied across the X-ray tube, the tube current that flows through the X-ray tube, and the total exposure time during which the current flows.

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Production -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: X-rays are a form of electromagnetic radiation with wavelengths ranging from 0.01 to 10 nanometers. In the setting of diagnostic radiology, X-rays have long enjoyed use in the imaging of body tissues and aid in the diagnosis of disease. Simply understood, the generation of X-rays occurs when electrons are accelerated under a potential difference and turned into electromagnetic radiation. [1] An X-ray tube, with its respective components placed in a vacuum, and a generator, make up the basic components of X-ray production. Essential components of an X-ray tube include a cathode, and an anode separated a short distance from each other, a vacuum enclosure, and high voltage cables forming the X-ray generator attached to the cathode and anode components. [2] In the generation of X-ray production, a cathode filament machined in a cathode cup is activated, causing intense heating of the cathode filament. [3] The heating of the filament leads to the release of electrons in a process called thermionic emission. [4] The released electrons form in an electron cloud at the filament surface, and repulsion forces prevent the ejection of electrons from this negatively charged cloud. [2] Upon application of a high voltage by an X-ray generator to the cathode as well as the anode, there is an acceleration of electrons ejected to an electrically positive anode. [3] The filament and the focusing cup determine this path of acceleration. The number of electrons is measured in the form of milliampere (mA) units, where 1 milliampere is equal to 6.24 x 10^15 electrons/s. Electron kinetic energy (measured in keV) is related to the applied voltage. The tube voltage, tube current, and exposure duration (measured in seconds) are adjustable by the user.

retrieved_docs.title: CT Instrumentation and Physics -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Tube Arcing: This equipment-induced artifact occurs due to short circuits within the x-ray tube, often caused by differences in electrical potential. [35] Aging tubes, changes in vacuum levels, or gas molecules within the tube can contribute to arcing. [35] It can lead to various artifacts, from minor streaks to severe image distortion. Reporting the issue to a service engineer for evaluation and potential resolution is essential when tube arcing occurs.

retrieved_docs.title: Safety of Fluoroscopy in Patient, Operator, and Technician  -- Function -- X-Ray Basics, Design and Function
retrieved_docs.content: An electrical current is used to generate X-rays from the X-ray tube via the creation of electrons directed at a tungsten anode which then converts the electrical energy and emits X-rays. [2] [3] This electrical energy can be modified before X-ray transmission via adjustments to the current or voltage potential across the cathode and anode in the X-ray tube to optimize the image generated. Increasing adjustments to the voltage potential (kVp) will ultimately deliver X-rays of a higher energy level, which allows for deeper penetration of tissue. In contrast, the number of X-ray emissions stays relatively stable.

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Image Acquisition -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: X-ray System Components - X-ray currents are measured in milliamperes(mA), while x-ray voltages are measured in kilovolt peak (kVp). [3] X-ray imaging systems generally comprise a few primary components: the operating console, the high-voltage generator, the x-ray tube, and the receiver (see Image. X-ray Generator). Operating consoles allow an operator to select a kVp, mA, and time. The console allows a technologist to establish an x-ray beam of adequate quantity (number of x-ray photons) and quality (effective energy of x-ray photons). Control of milliampere (mA), which constitutes the x-ray tube current traversing from the cathode to the anode, is monitored with an mA meter. The number of x-rays reaching an image receptor is also related to the time the tube is energized, initiated, and terminated by the radiographer.

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Image Acquisition -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Ceiling Support and the Vacuum Tube - The ceiling support system helps provide the primary support for an x-ray tube and serves as a protective apparatus. Among its many functions, the primary 1 is to avoid leakage and help in heat dissipation. The vacuum tube is composed of an anode and cathode within an enclosure. The cathode, which contains a tungsten filament, serves as the electron source, while a rotating anode, composed of a tungsten disc, serves as the electron target. [6] Tube failure has many causes, primarily excessive anode heating, anode cracking/pitting, and filament vaporization. Tube rating charts can help assess exposure levels and their relation to tube durability.

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Production Technical Evaluation -- Issues of Concern -- X-Ray Production
retrieved_docs.content: X-ray tubes are vacuum tubes used to convert input electrical power to X-rays. Essential components of the X-ray tube and housing include a negatively charged cathode filament, a positively charged anode target, a glass envelope, and lead tube housing. Producing X-rays in a tube generally requires 3 essential steps: the proper assembly of a tube with a source of electrons, a means to accelerate the electrons, and then decelerate the electrons.

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Image Acquisition -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Image Quality - Several factors determine image quality but are best characterized by a high signal-to-noise ratio (low noise) and good spatial resolution. Additional factors include factors relating to film processing, factors relating to geometric characteristics (such as magnification), and factors relating to the patient. The technologist has an important role in maintaining good image quality. For example, preventing patient movement helps reduce motion blur. [3] X-rays can either pass through a patient or are scattered by Compton interaction, which results in noise. Also, high kVp worsens the signal-to-noise ratio, while the thickness of the tissue being penetrated also increases noise. Using higher kVp results in an x-ray beam of higher average energy requiring a shorter exposure time and mA (together referred to as tube current exposure time product value or mAs), increasing patient penetration but also resulting in increased scatter and decreased image contrast. The converse is also true. A lower kVp setting requires increased mAs, resulting in decreased patient penetration, decreased scatter, and increased contrast. Carefully balancing these factors is essential for managing the relationship between patient dose, image contrast, and quality. Reduction of scatter radiation can be achieved by using grids, which have the added benefit of improving contrast and play an important role in producing an adequate quality and interpretable radiographic image. Although kVp increases noise, it also reduces patient dose. Collimators are devices that restrict/narrow beams and control scatters, resulting in increased signal-to-noise and decreased patient dose. In addition to image contrast, spatial resolution is an important measure of image quality. A smaller field of view results in a higher spatial resolution of the image and a decreased overall dose to the patient.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1990
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray is used as a reference point for several advanced computed tomography (CT) examinations. The CT scan of the chest is primarily mapped from the routine chest x‑ray, indicating it is the main derivative imaging study. In addition, CT examinations that include the brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium, as well as CT scans encompassing the brain, chest, and abdomen, are also linked to the routine chest x‑ray through other mappings, reflecting supplementary or alternative relationships. These connections illustrate how a basic chest radiograph can serve as a foundational study for more comprehensive, multi‑region CT imaging, sometimes enhanced with contrast agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1491
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray is a fundamental radiographic examination that utilizes plain X‑ray techniques as its method of acquisition. Building on this basic imaging approach, more advanced studies are conceptually derived from the routine chest x-ray. A computerized tomography of chest is directly mapped from the routine chest x-ray as its primary extension, providing cross‑sectional detail of thoracic structures. Additionally, a computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium represents another, more comprehensive variant that is also mapped from the routine chest x-ray, offering combined neuro‑and thoracic evaluation with enhanced contrast visualization. This hierarchy illustrates how basic plain X‑ray methods underpin increasingly sophisticated CT imaging modalities, guiding clinical assessment and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2850
retrieved_docs.content: Transmission imaging is linked to صورة مقطعية محوسبة, indicating that this imaging modality encompasses computed tomography scans. In clinical practice, صورة مقطعية محوسبة frequently co‑occurs with صورة نخاعية للمنطقة الشوكية, reflecting that CT imaging is often performed together with spinal‑region myelographic or spinal cord imaging to provide complementary anatomical detail for diagnosis and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 853
retrieved_docs.content: CT follow-up is classified as a Computed tomography imaging ‑ action and functions as a method of Unlisted computed tomography procedure (eg, diagnostic, interventional). This indicates that CT follow-up represents a specific imaging approach used when a standard listed CT procedure is not applicable, serving as a methodological option within broader diagnostic and interventional CT practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2105
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray serves as a foundational imaging reference that can be expanded into more detailed computed tomography (CT) examinations. It is the primary source from which a CT of the chest and abdomen and a CT of the chest are directly mapped, indicating a direct and principal relationship between the plain radiograph and these focused CT studies. Additionally, the routine chest x-ray is linked as an alternative or supplementary reference for broader CT examinations, including a computerized tomography of the brain and chest as well as a computerized tomography of the brain, chest, and abdomen. These mappings illustrate how a basic chest x-ray can inform and guide the selection of more comprehensive CT imaging of multiple thoracic and cranial regions, supporting nuanced diagnostic decision‑making and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1886
retrieved_docs.content: these compounds function as “allowed qualifiers” in the context of ionizing radiation because they can modify biological responses to radiation—either enhancing radiation‑induced damage, altering tissue radiosensitivity, or providing radioprotective or radiosensitizing effects. Awareness of these dual roles informs the integration of the agents into diagnostic imaging, radiotherapy planning, occupational radiation safety and broader radioprotection strategies, thereby optimizing therapeutic outcomes and patient safety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2416
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray serves as a foundational imaging reference for several computed tomography (CT) examinations. It is the primary source from which a dedicated Computerized tomography of chest is mapped, indicating a direct relationship between the standard chest radiograph and the more detailed CT chest study. In addition, the routine chest x-ray is also linked, through other mappings, to more extensive CT examinations that combine multiple anatomical regions: Computerized tomography of brain and chest, Computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium, and Computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen with intravenous contrast medium. These connections illustrate that the routine chest x-ray not only underpins isolated chest CT imaging but also informs broader, multi‑region CT protocols, both with and without the use of intravenous contrast, highlighting its central role in guiding advanced diagnostic imaging strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2375
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray is performed using plain X‑ray techniques. Computerized tomography of chest is considered the primary imaging modality that is mapped from the routine chest x‑ray, indicating that chest CT often follows or expands upon findings from a standard chest radiograph. In addition, computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen is another imaging study that is also mapped from the routine chest x‑ray, representing an alternative or supplementary advanced imaging approach that can be pursued when a broader anatomic assessment is required. These relationships illustrate how plain X‑ray techniques underlie routine chest x‑ray, which in turn serves as a reference point for both focused chest CT and more extensive CT examinations of the brain, chest, and abdomen.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2125
retrieved_docs.content: Radionuclide imaging – action is identified as a method of other nuclear medicine imaging, indicating that this imaging technique is employed as one of the various approaches within the broader field of nuclear medicine to visualize physiological and biochemical processes in patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 516
retrieved_docs.content: CT angiography of thorax with contrast, Computed tomography, thorax, diagnostic; without contrast material, followed by contrast material(s) and further sections, Computerized axial tomography (CT) scan, التصوير المقطعي المحوسب للصدر, Other chest x‑ray and the category “ct scan chest” are interrelated components of thoracic imaging. The “ct scan chest” category represents chest computed tomography and includes specific techniques such as CT angiography of the thorax with contrast, which is used to visualize thoracic vascular structures, and the diagnostic thoracic CT protocol that begins without contrast and proceeds with contrast-enhanced sections. Computerized axial tomography (CT) scan is the broader imaging modality that has the child “التصوير المقطعي المحوسب للصدر” (chest CT), positioning chest CT as a specialized subset of general CT scanning. In turn, chest CT serves as a parent category for both the specialized CT angiography and the diagnostic thoracic CT protocol, as well as for Other chest x‑ray, indicating that Other chest x‑ray is considered a related chest imaging study within the same hierarchical framework. This organized hierarchy clarifies how general CT technology narrows down to chest‑specific applications and how various chest imaging modalities are grouped for clinical decision‑making and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2605
retrieved_docs.content: The routine chest X-ray functions as a central reference point that links both advanced imaging studies and clinical encounters. A CT of chest, abdomen and pelvis is primarily mapped from the routine chest X‑ray, indicating that the plain‑film examination often guides the decision to obtain this comprehensive computed tomography scan. In a similar way, a Computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen with intravenous contrast medium is listed as another mapping derived from the routine chest X‑ray, reflecting its role in extending diagnostic evaluation beyond the initial radiograph. Clinically, an Encounter due to routine chest X‑ray is directly associated with the routine chest X‑ray itself, establishing a specific relationship between the patient visit and the imaging order. This encounter is also categorized as an Unspecified examination with a broader relationship, suggesting that while the primary focus is the chest X‑ray, the encounter may encompass additional, less defined assessments. Together, these connections illustrate how a routine chest X‑ray serves as a pivotal trigger for more detailed CT imaging and as a focal element of patient encounters within the healthcare workflow.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2941
retrieved_docs.content: CT follow-up is identified as a method_of, meaning it serves as a method of Computed tomography imaging - action, providing a structured approach for using CT scans in follow‑up assessments.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1938
retrieved_docs.content: CT of chest, abdomen and pelvis, Computerized tomography of chest, and Computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen (both with and without intravenous contrast medium) are imaging procedures that generate a computerized tomographic image (صورة مقطعية محوسبة). Each of these CT examinations is linked to the routine chest x‑ray as a source reference: the CT of chest, abdomen and pelvis and the Computerized tomography of chest are primarily mapped from the routine chest x‑ray, indicating a direct derivation or extension of the standard chest radiograph, whereas the Computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen and its contrast‑enhanced variant are mapped from the routine chest x‑ray in an “other” capacity, reflecting an alternative or supplementary relationship. This mapping underscores the routine chest x‑ray’s role as a foundational imaging study that can be expanded into more detailed cross‑sectional CT assessments, all of which produce the same type of output—a computerized tomographic image (صورة مقطعية محوسبة).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2519
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray serves as a basic imaging modality, and both computerized tomography of brain and chest and computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium are considered alternative or mapped imaging studies derived from the routine chest x-ray, indicating that these CT examinations may be employed for more detailed evaluation when the findings from a standard chest x-ray are insufficient.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1921
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray serves as a foundational imaging study that can be directly linked to several more advanced computed tomography (CT) examinations. It is primarily mapped to both “CT of chest, abdomen and pelvis” and “Computerized tomography of chest,” indicating that these comprehensive and focused chest CT scans are the main follow‑up modalities when further anatomic detail is required beyond the plain radiograph. Additionally, routine chest x-ray is associated with other, less primary CT options, including “Computerized tomography of brain and chest” and “Computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium.” These alternative mappings reflect situations where clinicians may extend evaluation to include intracranial structures or require contrast enhancement for enhanced tissue characterization. Together, these relationships illustrate how a routine chest x-ray can guide the selection of targeted or extensive CT imaging, supporting diagnostic decision‑making and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2131
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray serves as a reference point for several more advanced computed tomography examinations. Both Computerized tomography of brain and chest and Computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium are considered alternative mappings derived from a routine chest x-ray, indicating that these comprehensive CT studies may be ordered when a standard chest x-ray is insufficient. Additionally, Computerized tomography of chest is identified as the primary mapping from a routine chest x-ray, reflecting its role as the most direct CT extension of the basic chest radiograph. Understanding these relationships helps clinicians select appropriate imaging modalities, progressing from a routine chest x-ray to targeted CT imaging of the chest alone or combined brain‑chest assessment, with or without contrast, based on diagnostic needs.
